narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application. Your background also includes a middle-income family and plans to attend part-time, which is less common as only about 7% of students do so compared to the vast majority who go full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 55.2%. This tipped the scales just enough for your application to be rejected, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of around 5.5 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. While your undergraduate GPA played a significant counterbalancing role by strongly favoring a pass prediction—thanks to its above-average strength—your LSAT score provided a moderate positive push in the same direction, though not enough to fully offset the Year 3 concern. In essence, your earlier college and test strengths were overshadowed by that dip in later high school rankings, which carried the heaviest weight in the model's eyes compared to typical students who maintain steadier decile progress. This pattern sets you apart from many successful applicants, whose Year 3 deciles tend to align closer to the middle of their class distributions. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building recent academic momentum to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through community college or targeted prep programs that demonstrate upward trends. Strengthening your full-time commitment potential could also help, as part-time paths correlate with higher risk in the data. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on targeted improvements, and explore bridge programs or alternative law schools that value your GPA and LSAT highs while giving you a chance to prove your Year 3 performance was an outlier. With focused effort on those key areas, you can reshape your profile for a stronger prediction next time.
